coredata.dc:title: Waterborne polyurethane adhesives grafted with carbon dots for enhancing dispersion, cohesion, and adhesion
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Adhesion and Adhesives
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wan, Jing
Ye, Daiyong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) possess abundant functional groups on their surfaces and can be easily modified, allowing for covalent bonding with waterborne polyurethane (WPU) to create eco-friendly adhesives that exhibit both high cohesion and strong adhesion. However, as a type of nanofiller, CDs often aggregate, hindering effective dispersion. To address this dispersion challenge and enhance the properties of WPU adhesives, CDs were chemically grafted onto the macromolecular chains of WPUs using a silane coupling agent (KH550) as a linking agent. This method improved the adhesion strength of the resulting WPU nanocomposite adhesives, demonstrating the scientific validity of the approach. The successful grafting was confirmed through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and energy-dispersive spectroscopy. Optimizing the material ratios between the silane coupling agent and CDs significantly reduced CD aggregation in the WPU adhesives, as shown by scanning electron microscopy images that indicated efficient dispersion of the CDs. Malvern particle size analysis and thermogravimetric analysis revealed that when the CDs-to-KH550 mass ratio was 1:10, the water dispersion particle size of silane-modified CDs (KCD) decreased from 447.7 nm to 160.0 nm, and the total weight loss reduced from 53.07 % to 35.55 %. Furthermore, the adhesion properties of WPU adhesives modified with KCDs (KCD-WPU) were notably enhanced. The U-peel strength of the KCD-WPU adhesive on the steel test sheet increased by 31.8 %–510 N m−1 when the KCD content was 0.1 wt.%. The tensile strength of the KCD-WPU adhesive was 62.6 % greater than that of the original WPU adhesive. We proposed a mechanism for the improvement of properties in WPU adhesives grafted with a small amount of CDs. This research presents an effective solution to the agglomeration of CDs in WPU adhesives, thereby enhancing both cohesive and adhesive strength. This study lays the groundwork for designing high-performance WPU adhesives.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Silanization
Waterborne polyurethanes
Nanocomposite adhesives

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by a grant from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2022YFC3701702).The authors would like to express their gratitude to the analysis and testing centers at both the South China University of Technology and the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering for their support and assistance.
This research was financially supported by a grant from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2022YFC3701702 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: KCD-WPU prepared with different contents of KCD.
SEM images: (a) SEM image of CD; (b) SEM image of KCD; (c) Particle size distribution of CD; (d) Particle size distribution of KCD.
Malvern particle size distribution of KCD synthesized with different reaction ratios.
(a) TGA curves for CD and KCD; (b) DTG curves for some KCDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of CD, KH550, KCD; (b) FTIR spectra of WPU and KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD).

(a) XPS survey spectra of CD and KCD; (b) (c) (d) high-resolution XPS spectra of elements C, N, O for CD; (e) (f) (g) (h) high-resolution XPS spectra of elements C, N, O, Si for KCD; (i) XPS survey spectra of KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD).
XRD patterns of WPU and KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD).
U-shaped peel strength of nanocomposite waterborne adhesive tapes on ABS and steel plates: (a)/(b) ABS plate; (c)/(d) steel plate.
(a) Tensile curves of WPU and CD-WPU; (b) Tensile curves of WPU and KCD-WPU.
Mechanisms of interaction between KCDs and WPUs.
TGA curve (a) and DTG curve (b) of WPU and KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD).

(a) Water contact angle of WPU adhesive film; (b) Water contact angle of CD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for CD) adhesive film; (c) Water contact angle of KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD) adhesive film; (d) Contact angle of KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD) on ABS plates; (e) Contact angle of KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD) on steel plates.
SEM images: (a) cross-section of WPU adhesive film; (b) cross-section of KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD) composite adhesive film.
Silanization process on the surface of carbon dots.
Ingredients for preparation of modified CDs.
Average particle sizes of different KCD samples.
Temperatures at which 20 (T20 %) and 40 (T40 %) wt.% loss occurs in KCD.
EDS analysis of different KCD samples.
Tensile strength and elongation at break of WPU, CD-WPU, and KCD-WPU.
Properties of KCD-WPU dispersions and films prepared with different KCD contents.

head.ce:title: Waterborne polyurethane adhesives grafted with carbon dots for enhancing dispersion, cohesion, and adhesion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510640, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) possess abundant functional groups on their surfaces and can be easily modified, allowing for covalent bonding with waterborne polyurethane (WPU) to create eco-friendly adhesives that exhibit both high cohesion and strong adhesion. However, as a type of nanofiller, CDs often aggregate, hindering effective dispersion. To address this dispersion challenge and enhance the properties of WPU adhesives, CDs were chemically grafted onto the macromolecular chains of WPUs using a silane coupling agent (KH550) as a linking agent. This method improved the adhesion strength of the resulting WPU nanocomposite adhesives, demonstrating the scientific validity of the approach. The successful grafting was confirmed through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and energy-dispersive spectroscopy. Optimizing the material ratios between the silane coupling agent and CDs significantly reduced CD aggregation in the WPU adhesives, as shown by scanning electron microscopy images that indicated efficient dispersion of the CDs. Malvern particle size analysis and thermogravimetric analysis revealed that when the CDs-to-KH550 mass ratio was 1:10, the water dispersion particle size of silane-modified CDs (KCD) decreased from 447.7 nm to 160.0 nm, and the total weight loss reduced from 53.07 % to 35.55 %. Furthermore, the adhesion properties of WPU adhesives modified with KCDs (KCD-WPU) were notably enhanced. The U-peel strength of the KCD-WPU adhesive on the steel test sheet increased by 31.8 %–510 N m−1 when the KCD content was 0.1 wt.%. The tensile strength of the KCD-WPU adhesive was 62.6 % greater than that of the original WPU adhesive. We proposed a mechanism for the improvement of properties in WPU adhesives grafted with a small amount of CDs. This research presents an effective solution to the agglomeration of CDs in WPU adhesives, thereby enhancing both cohesive and adhesive strength. This study lays the groundwork for designing high-performance WPU adhesives.

ce:keyword.ce:text: carbon dots modified by γ-aminopropyl triethoxysilane

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are typically composed of quasi-spherical nanoparticles with an average size of <10 nm [1,2]. They feature a distinctive carbon core structure, a high density of surface functional groups, and remarkable multifunctional properties. Moreover, they are straightforward to synthesize, biocompatible, and easily functionalized [3–8]. Various methods have been employed to synthesize CDs, including microwave synthesis, hydrothermal processes, combustion, laser ablation, and electrochemical stripping [9–13]. These characteristics provide CDs with a wide range of chemical structures and applications, such as sensing, drug delivery, photocatalysis, anticounterfeiting, fluorescence, and cellular imaging [14–19]. Given these diverse applications, polyurethanes serve as excellent substrates for chemical modification with CDs. Polyurethanes are particularly advantageous due to their ease of processing, low specific gravity, ability to be modified for altered physical properties, and flexibility [20]. When CDs are utilized to reinforce polyurethane matrices [21–24], two primary methods are employed: chemical reactions involving covalent or ionic interactions, or physical adsorption [25]. For polymeric adhesives or coatings, the unique properties of CDs provide significant potential benefits, including enhanced mechanical properties [22,26], corrosion resistance [27], superior luminescence, bioactivity, and hydrophobicity [28–31].

However, the high surface energy of CDs and the issue of agglomeration caused by self-π–π stacking interactions between nanoparticles impede the uniform dispersion of CDs within the polyurethane matrix, leading to diminished mechanical strength and unstable optical properties in the polymeric composites. Additionally, the migration of CDs from the composites may be attributed to the predominant presence of hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, or π–π interactions between CDs and the polymer matrix [32–34]. As a result, surface modification of CDs can be employed to improve their dispersion and compatibility with polymers. Elsayed et al. [35] utilized a combination of organic semiconducting polymers and N-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to create heterostructured photocatalysts in the form of polymer dots. The strong interactions between these materials enhanced the stability of the polymer dot structure. Furthermore, silylation modification, which involves a reaction between silane and active hydrogen on the surface of CDs, helped maintain the specific surface area of CDs and facilitated their dispersion [36]. Rao et al. [37] synthesized CDs–NH2 using a hydrothermal method and encapsulated them in a silica shell through a silylation reaction. This modification achieved a favorable balance between inert and active functional groups, minimizing particle aggregation and nonspecific binding.

One potential strategy for utilizing CDs in adhesives is the development of nanocomposite adhesives combining CDs with waterborne polyurethane (WPU). WPUs are advantageous due to their environmentally friendly, nontoxic, nonflammable nature, and low volatile organic compound (VOC) content, which has led to extensive research and application in coatings, adhesives, and inks. They also exhibit excellent low-temperature resistance, strong adhesion, and superior mechanical properties [38–42]. The aromatic carbon structure of CDs, along with their highly polar functional groups, ensures strong physical interactions and chemical reactions with the aqueous polyurethane matrix, thereby enhancing the mechanical properties and thermal stability of WPU. Additionally, certain optical properties are imparted to the composites. Qiang et al. [43] prepared CQDs-WPU nanocomposites through in-situ polymerization, demonstrating that CQDs-WPU nanocomposites outperformed pure WPU. These nanocomposites exhibited improved elongation at break and thermal stability compared to pure WPU. Agnol et al. [44] developed a photoactive coating based on CQDs-WPU, which effectively prevented the formation of bacterial biofilms. The WPU/CQDs nanocomposites displayed superior mechanical, rheological, and thermal properties compared to pure WPU.

This research presents an effective method for modifying CDs using a silane coupling agent for WPU adhesives, optimizing the mass ratio of the silane coupling agent to CDs, and addressing the agglomeration issue of CDs in WPUs. A novel, straightforward, and effective method was employed to graft CDs onto the macromolecular chains of WPU, with the silane coupling agent acting as a bridge. This research resulted in enhanced cohesive strength, peel strength, and thermal stability of the WPU adhesives. Upon examining the effect of by γ-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (KH550)–modified CDs (KCD) on adhesive properties, it was found that incorporating a minimal amount of CDs led to a significant improvement in the properties of WPU adhesives. Furthermore, the high dispersibility of KCD facilitated greater synergy with WPU adhesives, in contrast to the diminished performance of CDs due to the agglomeration phenomenon in WPU adhesives. This research provides an effective solution to the agglomeration of CDs in WPU adhesives, thereby enhancing the cohesive and adhesion strength of WPU adhesives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid (CA) was acquired from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Science and Technology Co., Ltd., China. Polypropylene glycol (PPG) was sourced from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTDL) was obtained from Shandong Keyuan Biochemistry Co., Ltd., China. All chemicals mentioned in this section were of chemically pure grade.

Ethylenediamine (EDA) was procured from Chengdu Colony Chemicals Co., Ltd., China. Anhydrous ethanol and triethylamine (TEA) were supplied by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory, China. Butane-1,4-diol (BDO) was obtained from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Acetone (AC) was sourced from Guangdong Guanghua Reagent Technology Co., Ltd., China. KH550 was purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Science and Technology Co. All chemicals listed in this paragraph were of analytically pure grade.

Toluene diisocyanate (TDI) was acquired from Bayer Material Science (China) Ltd., China. 2,2-Bis(hydroxymethyl)propionic acid (DMPA) was sourced from Perstor, Sweden. An aqueous rosin tackifier emulsion was obtained from Shenzhen Jitian Chemical Co., Ltd., China. All chemicals mentioned in this paragraph were of industrial grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of KCD
Synthesis of WPUs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of WPU was carried out following a specific procedure. All materials, except for TDI, were subjected to moisture removal using either a vacuum drying oven or 4A zeolite adsorbents. A total of 38.28 g (19.14 mmol) of PPG 2000, 10.00 g (57.42 mmol) of TDI, and 3 drops of DBTDL were added to a four-neck round-bottom flask equipped with a stirrer and a condenser. The temperature was raised to 75 °C for 2–3 h. Next, 1.69 g (12.6 mmol) of the hydrophilic chain extender, DMPA, was dispersed in AC and introduced into the flask, with the temperature increased to 85°C-90 °C. The small molecule chain extender BDO was added at a mass of 0.8 g (8.77 mmol), and the reaction continued at 80 °C for 1 h. The reactants were then cooled to room temperature, and 1.1 g (7.37 mmol) of TEA was added, resulting in the formation of the prepolymer, which was stirred for 30 min. During the synthesis, AC was added to adjust the viscosity of the prepolymer. Finally, water was introduced under high-speed dispersion conditions at 2000 r/min for self-emulsification, and 0.5 g of EDA (8.319 mmol) was added to complete the chain extension, yielding the WPU. The WPU was then placed in a round-bottom flask for vacuum spin-distillation to remove VOCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of KCD-WPU composite adhesives
ce:section.ce:para: A series of specific mass fractions of KCD was added into the WPU (0.025–0.15 wt.%) to utilize the reactive groups on the KCD surface and the groups within the macromolecular chains of the WPU to form hydrogen bonds or covalent bonds. Initially, KCD was dispersed in 1 g of anhydrous ethanol and ultrasonically treated until no precipitation was observed. This KCD dispersion was then added to the WPU and magnetically stirred for 20 min. Following this, 10 wt.% of an aqueous rosin tackifier emulsion, 0.15 wt.% leveling agent, 0.1 wt.% antifoam agent, and 0.25 wt.% wetting agent were added. The WPU adhesives modified with silanized CDs were then ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min and transferred into sample bottles, as illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The adhesives were uniformly applied to PVC film according to the GB/T 2792-2014 standard, leveled, and dried at room temperature for 30 min. They were then placed in an oven at 50 °C for 6 h. Finally, the adhesive tape was pressed onto the substrate (ABS board or steel plate) using a manual pressure roller.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the impact of grafting KH550 on the structure and surface functional groups of the CDs’ nanoparticles, tests were conducted using a German Bruker Tensor 27 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; AXIS SUPRA+, Japan). FTIR was also employed to confirm the successful grafting of KCD with WPU. The stability and degree of grafting of KCD and KCD-WPU were evaluated using a Malvern ZS nanoparticle size analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd) and a HORIBA EMAX EDS (Hitachi SU 8220). The influence of KCD on the crystallinity of the soft segments of the WPU molecular chain was analyzed via X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a D8 advanced X-ray diffractometer (Bruker Analytical Instruments GmbH, Germany) with test voltages and currents set at 40 kV and 40 mA, respectively, and 2θ scans conducted in the range of 5°–60°. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed to evaluate the thermal stability of the materials using an SDTQ600 from the United States. The samples were heated at a rate of 10 °C/min, with a gas flow rate of 100 mL/min, across a temperature range of 30°C-600 °C, under a flowing nitrogen atmosphere. Nanoparticles were dispersed in AC at the same mass ratio with sonication, and the dispersion was subsequently dripped onto silicon wafers using a capillary tube. The films were dried and quenched with liquid nitrogen to achieve a flatter cross-section. The surface morphology of the nanoparticles and the cross-sectional morphology of the colloidal film were examined using cold field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

The properties of both the adhesives and films were measured according to procedures outlined in International Standards, Chinese National Standards, or Chinese Chemical Industry Standards. For the mechanical properties of the nanocomposite adhesives, the U-type peel strength and tensile strength were determined using a KESSTER intelligent tensile tester from Dongguan Ketai Instrument Co., Ltd. The mechanical properties of each material were evaluated according to the GB/T 2792–2014 and GB/T 1040.2–2006 test methods. The peel test was conducted at a loading rate of 5 mm/s, while the tensile test was performed at a rate of 1 mm/min, with three specimens of each material tested. Water resistance was assessed according to GB/T 4893.1–2005. Alcohol resistance and alkali resistance were tested according to HG/T 4759-2014. Calcium chloride stability was tested according to GB/T 20623-2006.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Improvement of dispersion and thermal stability of chemically modified CDs
SEM analysis of CD and KCD
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 presents the SEM images of the CDs and 1:10-KCD nanoparticles. The low dispersibility of the pristine CD, as shown in Fig. 2(a), results in a significant tendency for agglomeration. In contrast, Fig. 2(b) illustrates that the spatial stability provided by grafting KH550 greatly enhances the dispersion of KCD, resulting in a more uniform particle size distribution. The particle size distribution of the nanoparticles was analyzed using the Gaussian fitting method based on the SEM images (with the particle size distribution of CD derived from the unagglomerated portion). From the particle size distribution graph in Fig. 2(c), it is evident that the average particle size of the unagglomerated CD is 9.50 nm, while the average particle size of KCD increases to 18.54 nm. This increase is attributed to the coverage of the CDs’ surfaces with organo-silane network chains, which enlarges the size of the nanospherical particles. In addition to demonstrating the superior dispersibility of KCD through SEM images, the influence of KH550 content on the properties of KCD nanoparticles was further investigated through several additional tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Improvement of KCD dispersion demonstrated by Malvern particle size analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Under consistent conditions, Fig. 3 and Table 2 display the results of particle size tests conducted on various samples. The high electronegativity of the carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs leads to a strong attraction to surrounding molecules, which contributes to their tendency to agglomerate and results in low dispersibility, reflected in the broad particle size distribution of all tested samples. The average particle sizes of KCD show a gradual decrease and improvement in dispersibility as the amount of KCD added increases. The best dispersibility is observed at a ratio of 1:10. This improvement is due to the increasing degree of condensation between the silane coupling agent and the CDs, which enhances the stability of the CDs and effectively prevents particle agglomeration. However, at the 1:15 ratio, the high content of the silane coupling agent leads to increased side reactions of its own condensation, which diminishes the interaction between the hydrolysis products and the CDs. As a result, the average particle size increases again. The data also indicate an increase in the average particle size of KCD-WPU composite adhesives, attributed to the cross-linking among the chain segments facilitated by the reaction between the reactive groups on the KCD surface and the WPU groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Improvement of KCD thermal stability demonstrated by TGA analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The TGA curves for CD and various KCD samples are illustrated in Fig. 4 . The TGA curve for CD is primarily divided into two stages: Stage I corresponds to the removal of adsorbed H2O from the surface of the CD, while Stage II begins around 350 °C, indicating the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups and amino groups on its surface, resulting in a final weight loss of approximately 53.07 wt.%. In contrast, the TGA curve for KCD can be segmented into three stages. Stage I still pertains to the removal of adsorbed H2O on the nanoparticle surfaces [45]. In Stages II and III, it is evident that the weight loss of KCD decreases as the KH550 content increases, with the 1:10-KCD exhibiting the lowest weight loss at about 35.55 wt.%. This reduction in weight loss is primarily due to the silanated CDs being grafted with a silane network chain on their surfaces, which forms strong Si–O–Si and Si–O–C covalent bonds [46]. These bonds help to reduce and slow down the thermal degradation of the functional groups in the shell layer of the CD. Consequently, in Stage II, the weight loss of KCD is mainly attributed to the degradation of the silane chains. As some of the silane chains on the surface of KCD degrade, the internal functional groups, along with the mesh chain, begin to degrade and lose weight significantly in Stage III. Overall, the weight loss of KCD remains lower than that of CD. The derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) curves and Table 3 further indicate that the degradation rate of KCD gradually decreases with increasing grafting content of KH550.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elemental composition of KCD analyzed by EDS
ce:section.ce:para: Table 4 summarizes the elemental composition of the KCD nanoparticles obtained from energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analyses. KH550 serves as the sole source of elemental silicon (Si), and thus the Si content reflects the degree of KH550 linkage on the surface of the CD. It is observed that the overall Si content tends to increase with higher amounts of KH550, peaking at a mass ratio of 1:10. This finding aligns with the results from the Malvern average particle size test. However, the silanation reaction of CDs may compete with side reactions occurring after the hydrolysis of the silane coupling agent, which could lead to the Si content of the silanated CDs not following the anticipated increasing trend.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probing of grafting CDs onto macromolecular chains of WPU
Grafting of CDs demonstrated by FTIR analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The most well-dispersed and thermally stable 1:10-KCD was selected for further testing and will be referred to as KCD hereafter. The FTIR spectra of KCD and KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD) are presented in Fig. 5 . As illustrated in Fig. 5(a), the spectrum of pristine CD shows a broad peak near 3450 cm−1 associated with the stretching vibration of the O–H group, an absorption peak at 1556 cm−1 corresponding to the bending vibration of N–H, and a sharp peak at 1700 cm−1 resulting from the –C=O stretching vibration. In contrast, KCD retains the O–H stretching vibration peak at 3450 cm−1 and the –C=O stretching vibration peak at 1700 cm−1, while also enhancing the N–H bending vibration peak at 1556 cm−1. This enhancement is attributed to the grafting of the silane coupling agent on the CD surface, which introduces additional –NH3 groups. Most notably, the appearance of Si–O–Si stretching vibration absorption peaks at 1100 cm−1 for KCD confirms that the hydrolyzed silanol of KH550 condenses with the hydroxyl groups on the CD surface to form silicon–oxygen bonds, thereby validating the success of the grafting process. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the FTIR spectra of the WPU reveal an enhanced –C=O stretching vibration peak of KCD-WPU at 1700 cm−1, along with a new stretching vibration absorption peak of Si–O–Si at 1100 cm−1. This further indicates that the nanoparticles were successfully grafted into the WPU.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Grafting of CDs demonstrated by XPS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The surface phase structures of CD, KCD, and KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD) are further characterized in Fig. 6 . In the survey spectrum of CD (Fig. 6(a)), three peaks at 284.5 eV, 399.3 eV, and 531.6 eV correspond to C 1s (71.92 %), N 1s (8.74 %), and O 1s (19.34 %), respectively. In the survey spectrum of KCD (Fig. 6(a)), four peaks at 283.9 eV, 398.6 eV, 531.1 eV, and 100.2 eV are attributed to C 1s (68.41 %), N 1s (7.56 %), O 1s (18.86 %), and Si 2p (5.17 %). The relatively weak Si 2p peak is associated with the newly formed silanol groups on the surface of the CDs [47]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum in Fig. 6(g) reveals three peaks corresponding to Si–O, C=O, and C–OH/C–O–C at 530.8 eV, 531.9 eV, and 533.0 eV, respectively. The two peaks at 101.7 eV and 102.4 eV in the Si 2p spectrum of Fig. 6(h) relate to Si–C and Si–O bonds, respectively [48]. Additionally, the survey spectrum of KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD) is shown in Fig. 6(i). The binding energy peak of Si 2p appears in the survey spectrum of WPU, with an atomic occupancy of 4.75 % corresponding to this peak. This indicates that the CD was successfully grafted onto WPU through the bridging of KH550. The analytical results from this XPS spectral analysis align well with those from the FTIR absorption spectroscopic analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between KCD and WPU demonstrated by XRD analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns of WPU and KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD) are depicted in Fig. 7 . A broad diffraction peak associated with the soft segments of polyurethane is observed in WPU around 2θ = 20°. However, the intensity of the amorphous diffraction peaks in KCD-WPU diminishes following the incorporation of KCD. This reduction confirms that the addition of KCD promotes slight cross-linking of the WPU molecular chains, enhancing the microphase mixing of the soft and hard chain segments within the WPU. This mixing restricts the convergence and mobility of the polyurethane chain segments [49]. Consequently, this effect leads to a decrease in the crystallinity of the composites, resulting in a lower intensity of the diffraction peaks. The findings from this analysis indicate a significant interaction between KCD and WPU.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effective improvement of adhesion properties and cohesive energy of WPU adhesives by grafting CDs
U-peel strength of KCD-WPU adhesives
ce:section.ce:para: The U-peel strengths of the CD-WPU and KCD-WPU composite adhesives on various substrates are shown in Fig. 8 . A slight decrease in peel strength is observed for the composite adhesives after adding the original CD to the WPU, likely due to the presence of numerous CD agglomerates, which reduce the wettability between the waterborne adhesives and the substrate. In contrast, the addition of KCD leads to an increase in the peel strength of the KCD-WPU adhesive, primarily due to the enhanced spatial stability of the nanoparticles resulting from the grafting of silane groups. This results in a higher degree of dispersion and grafting of KCD within the WPU macromolecules. As illustrated in Fig. 8(b), when the KCD content is 0.1 wt.%, the peel strength of KCD-WPU reaches its peak at 402 N m−1 on the ABS plate, which is 32.2 % higher than that of the original WPU. Fig. 8(d) also shows that the peel strength is maximized at 0.1 wt.% of KCD on the steel plate (510 N m−1), representing a 31.8 % increase compared to the pristine WPU. However, further increases in KCD content do not enhance the peel strength of the adhesive, indicating that the interaction strength between the nanoparticles and the WPU polymer chains has approached saturation. When the KCD addition is below 0.1 wt.%, the peel strength of KCD-WPU adhesives shows a positive correlation with KCD content. This is attributed to the increasing degree of reaction between KCD and the WPU polymer chains, which enhances the cohesive strength of the adhesive.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tensile strength of CD-WPU and KCD-WPU adhesive films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 and Table 5 present the tensile test results for the composite films. The tensile strengths of all CD-WPU films did not show significant changes compared to those of the WPU films without CDs. In contrast, as shown in Fig. 9(b), the tensile strength of all KCD-WPU films increased. When the KCD content was below 0.1 w.t%, the tensile strength of the films improved with increasing KCD content. Additionally, the tensile strength of the composite film with 0.1 wt.% KCD increased by 62.6 % to 3.871 MPa compared to the WPU film. These results align with the U-peel strength findings, and the enhancement of the cohesion in the composite films is also attributed to the chemical interactions between KCD and WPU.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on the mechanism of property enhancement in WPU adhesives grafted with low levels of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As is well known, adhesive failure can be categorized into interfacial damage, adhesive damage, and mixing damage. Therefore, the cohesive strength of an adhesive significantly influences its bonding strength, particularly when the wetting conditions between the adhesive and substrate are favorable. The molecular chain segments of WPU consist of a combination of soft and hard segments linked by covalent bonds. The soft segments are derived from various long-chain diols or polyols, while the hard segments are formed through the condensation of small molecule chain extenders with –NCO groups. Consequently, the –OH or –NH2 groups present on the surface of KCD can chemically react with the unreacted –NCO groups of the WPUs, as well as form hydrogen bonding interactions with groups such as carbamate groups. As illustrated in Fig. 10 , the hydrogen bonds or chemical bonds between KCD and WPU contribute to enhancing the cohesive strength of the composite adhesive, which in turn improves the adhesive and mechanical properties of the material. During the mixing process, the dispersion of CDs influences the degree of reaction between the CDs and the polymeric WPU chains. Regarding the enhancement of properties in WPU adhesives grafted with low levels of CDs, KH550 not only improves the dispersion of CDs in WPU but also acts as a bridging agent by grafting onto the surface of the CDs and reacting with the WPU. These two synergistic effects strengthen the interaction between the CDs and WPU macromolecules, thereby effectively enhancing the performance of the WPU adhesive.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other properties of WPU adhesive films
Thermal properties of KCD-WPU adhesive films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 presents the TGA and DTG curves for WPU and KCD-WPU (0.1 wt.% for KCD). The degradation of both the WPU adhesive film and the KCD-WPU adhesive film occurs in two main stages. The first stage primarily involves the degradation of hard segments (i.e., urea and carbamate bonds), while the second stage pertains to the degradation of soft segments (i.e., polyester or polyether). From the DTG curves, it is evident that the maximum degradation temperature of the hard segments in WPU slightly increases from 304 °C to 310 °C after modification, while the temperature for the soft segments remains nearly unchanged. Additionally, the TG curves indicate that the weight loss of the KCD-WPU adhesive film is reduced by approximately 2.252 wt.% compared to the original WPU adhesive film, suggesting a slight enhancement in the thermal stability of KCD-WPU.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angles of KCD-WPU adhesive films
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 12 (a)–(c), the incorporation of CDs leads to an increase in the hydrophilicity of the aqueous polyurethane adhesive film due to the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs. Conversely, the addition of KCDs enhances the hydrophobicity of the nanocomposite adhesive film. This is attributed to the grafting of KH550 on the surface of the CDs, which counteracts the effects of the hydrophilic groups [47].

According to the Young-Dupre equation (W
a : adhesion work; γ
LV : liquid-vapor interfacial tension; θ: contact angle/wetting angle), W
a = γ
LV (1 + cos θ), where γ
LV is a constant value when the liquid is constant. Therefore, a smaller contact angle θ is results in a larger adhesion work W
a . Consequently, it can be theoretically inferred from Fig. 12(d) and (e) that the adhesion work of KCD-WPU on steel is slightly greater than that on ABS, which aligns with the results from the U-peel strength tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cross-sectional morphology of WPU and KCD-WPU adhesive films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 13 presents the cross-sectional electron micrographs of the WPU colloidal film and the KCD-WPU composite colloidal film. The cross-section of the WPU adhesive film appears relatively smooth, while the KCD-WPU composite adhesive film exhibits a uniform dispersion of nanoparticles along with numerous small raised holes. This phenomenon is attributed to the enhanced cohesion of the composite adhesive film, which requires the KCD to overcome greater forces when being pulled away from the WPU, resulting in the formation of these pore-like structures in the adhesive film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of KCD-WPU dispersions and films
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table 6 , the incorporation of KCD does not significantly alter the properties of KCD-WPU, including solvent resistance, stability, and pH. However, the introduction of KCD facilitates the formation of strong interactions between the nanoparticles and the molecular chains of WPU, leading to an increase in the average particle size of the WPU. Additionally, the polydispersity index (PDI) serves as a crucial metric for assessing the uniformity of particle size distribution. The modified –NH2 and –COOH groups on the surface of KCD can establish directional hydrogen bonds with the WPU matrix, thereby enhancing dispersion uniformity and resulting in a slight reduction in the PDI of the dispersions. Nevertheless, as the concentration of nanoparticles continues to rise, local agglomeration occurs, which causes an increase in the PDI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In conclusion, the chemically modified CDs using KH550 have been shown to enhance the dispersion of CD nanoparticles within WPU adhesives. The morphology of both CD and KCD nanoparticles was examined using SEM, revealing an average particle size of KCD at 18.54 nm. The improvements in dispersion and thermal stability of the CDs were further confirmed through Malvern particle size analysis and TGA testing. Optimal dispersibility and thermal stability of KCDs were achieved at a mass ratio of CDs-to-KH550 of 1:10. Under these conditions, the water-dispersed particle size of the nanoparticles decreased from 447.7 nm to 160.0 nm, while thermal weight loss reduced from 53.07 % to 35.55 %. Furthermore, the successful grafting of KCD onto WPU and the interactions between KCD and WPU were validated through FTIR, XPS, and XRD analyses. The U-peel strengths of KCD-WPU nanocomposite adhesives with varying KCD contents on ABS and steel plates were also examined. As KCD content increased, the peel strengths initially rose before subsequently declining. At a KCD concentration of 0.1 wt.%, the peel strength of the composite on the steel plate reached 510 N m−1, which is 31.8 % higher than that of the original WPU. Additionally, the tensile strength tests of the KCD-WPU films aligned well with the U-peel strength results. We also discussed the mechanisms behind the property enhancements of the WPU adhesives grafted with low levels of KCD. Finally, other properties of the nanocomposite adhesive films were evaluated, including contact angle and thermal stability tests. This study demonstrated that the addition of a small quantity of KCD to WPU adhesives results in excellent dispersibility and improved adhesive strength, establishing a solid strategy for designing high-performance, eco-friendly adhesives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Jing Wan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Daiyong Ye: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Funding
ce:section.ce:para: This research was financially supported by a grant from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2022YFC3701702).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude to the analysis and testing centers at both the South China University of Technology and the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering for their support and assistance.

Coatings
